trajectory_summary: The call opens with a soft lead who is genuinely pre-decisional and ends with a vague callback promise and no appointment. Meg is pleasant and competent on rapport but never attempts to create curiosity or reframe the value of a design conversation as a planning tool for Deb's situation. The warmest energy on the call arrives after the commercial conversation has already closed, which means the rapport was built too late to do any work.
outcome_dispute: No prior outcome tag was provided for this call. The observable outcome is a soft callback with no locked time, no appointment structure, and no qualification completed — this should be tagged as soft_callback / no_appointment, not as a booked sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Reframing the design conversation as the planning tool — not the sales pitch
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, I'm not really ready to do this. That's why I just wanted the estimate.
primary_coaching_focus.why: On this call, Meg accepted Deb's 'not ready' framing at first contact and never tested it again. Deb was not hostile — she was pre-decisional and actively curious (she asked an unsolicited question about roof sequencing at 01:47). The design conversation was the exact tool Deb said she wanted; Meg never made that connection explicit. The coaching move is the objection_inversion reframe: the appointment IS the estimate. Until Meg can make that pivot, she will lose every pre-decisional lead at first contact.

key_moments.quote: I'm okay. I'm like, who's calling me from South Carolina?
key_moments.stewart_read: Deb's opening question is a mild legitimacy challenge — she doesn't know who Ion is or why a South Carolina number is calling her. Meg responds with 'Yep. That's me. So sorry about that.' — an apology rather than a legitimacy anchor. The codex's intros.standard coaching note calls for referencing the form submission to legitimize the call; Meg does mention the form in her intro but then apologizes for the caller ID rather than using it as a trust moment ('You filled out a form about solar — that's how I have your info, and that's my work line calling from our office'). The apology is not damaging, but it's a missed micro-trade on a customer who is already mildly suspicious.

key_moments.quote: Well, I'm not really ready to do this. That's why I just wanted the estimate.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's pivotal moment. Deb's 'not ready' framing is a pre-decisional deflection, not a hard objection — she filled out the form, she's buying a house, she wants to understand solar. The codex's objection_inversion principle applies: the flip available here is that the design conversation IS the estimate she wants. Meg instead responds 'For sure. Okay.' and pivots to logistics. Strategic read: Meg may have judged that pushing on a pre-close customer would create friction. Reactive read: Meg accepted the customer's frame without testing whether curiosity could be created. The reactive read is more supported — Meg never attempts any reframe across the full call, not just here.

key_moments.quote: So is it better to go ahead and have a roof replaced, or is it better to wait and do that at the same time you're gonna install panels?
key_moments.stewart_read: Deb is asking an unsolicited, specific question about roof-and-solar sequencing — this is a genuine engagement signal from a customer who just said she wasn't ready. She is thinking through the project, not deflecting. This is the highest-value moment in the call commercially: Deb has a house from the 1940s, an almost-certain roof replacement need, and is actively asking how to coordinate it with solar. The codex's cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned template applies directly.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. We also are able to do roof replacements here at Ion. We do, like, a bundle option. So if that's something we need to do, you know, we could always explore that when I call you back in a couple weeks.
key_moments.stewart_read: Meg is aware of the bundle and names it — this is not a rep who missed the product entirely. The gap is in execution: she defers the roofing conversation to the May callback rather than routing to the roofing team in parallel now. A 1940s house with an uninspected roof and a customer actively asking about sequencing is a roofing lead today, independent of whether solar closes in May. The alternative hypothesis — that routing is premature given Deb hasn't closed on the house yet — has some merit, but the codex's cross_sell_signals section explicitly calls for co-routing to the roofing team in real time. A roofing consultation doesn't require Deb to own the house yet; it requires her interest, which she has demonstrated.
